title: Why ideologies matter
content: Political ideologies are frameworks for answering big questions: what is freedom, what is fairness, and what should government do. Learning them helps you compare arguments by principles, not just slogans.
next: What tensions appear in almost every political debate?

title: Three tensions that organize politics
content: Most ideologies prioritize values differently across three recurring tensions: freedom versus equality, tradition versus reform, and limited state versus strong state. These trade-offs explain why reasonable people can support different systems.
next: Which ideology puts tradition and stability first?

title: Conservatism: preserve and reform carefully
content: Conservatism emphasizes continuity, social order, and respect for established institutions. It favors gradual change over abrupt transformation, arguing that sudden redesign can produce instability and unintended harm.
next: What if your first priority is individual rights?

title: Liberalism: rights and constrained power
content: Liberalism centers individual rights, legal equality, and limits on arbitrary authority. Classical liberalism stressed civil liberties and markets; modern liberalism also emphasizes social protections that expand real opportunity.

title: Socialism: equality and shared control
content: Socialism argues that concentrated ownership creates unfair outcomes and power imbalances. It supports broader social or collective control over key resources so prosperity is distributed more evenly across society.
next: How is communism related but more radical?

title: Communism: classless ideal, difficult transitions
content: Communism is often described as a classless system with common ownership and no exploitation. The historical challenge has been implementation: many self-described communist states became highly centralized, creating tension between ideal theory and political reality.
next: What happens when nationalism and authoritarianism merge?

title: Fascism: authoritarian ultranationalism
content: Fascism elevates nation and leader above pluralism, dissent, and individual rights. It rejects liberal democracy and often uses propaganda, repression, and exclusionary identity politics to enforce unity.
next: Are all anti-state ideas authoritarian in a different way?

title: Anarchism: no coercive hierarchy
content: Anarchism rejects coercive authority structures, especially state domination. It proposes voluntary cooperation, mutual aid, and self-management, though anarchist schools disagree on markets and property.
next: Which ideology also seeks minimal government but keeps markets central?

title: Libertarianism: maximize individual choice
content: Libertarianism prioritizes personal liberty, private property, and limited government intervention. Supporters argue this protects autonomy and innovation, while critics argue it can weaken social safety nets and public goods.
next: How do nationalism and populism shape modern political energy?

title: Identity, sovereignty, and anti-elite politics
content: Nationalism prioritizes national identity and self-rule; it can be civic and inclusive, or exclusionary and ethnic. Populism frames politics as ordinary people versus entrenched elites and can appear on both left and right.
next: What changed when movements centered gender and climate?

title: Feminism and environmentalism broaden the agenda
content: Feminism analyzes and challenges systems that reproduce gender inequality in law, culture, and economics. Environmentalism treats ecological limits and climate risk as central political responsibilities, reshaping policy across ideological camps.
next: How should beginners use the left-right-center spectrum?

title: Use labels as tools, not cages
content: Left-right-center is a useful orientation map, but real parties and voters mix ideas from multiple traditions. The deeper skill is identifying each ideology's priorities, trade-offs, and likely social outcomes.
next: Test your foundations before moving to historical case studies.
